lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Cousins, I hope the days are near at hand

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: That chambers will be safe.

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: We doubt it nothing.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: What wood is this before us?

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: The wood of Birnam.

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Let every soldier hew him down a bough

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: And bear’t before him: thereby shall we shadow

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: The numbers of our host and make discovery

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Err in report of us.

lines.speaker: SOLDIERS
lines.text: It shall be done.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: We learn no other but the confident tyrant

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Keeps still in Dunsinane, and will endure

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Our setting down before’t.

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: ’Tis his main hope:

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: For where there is advantage to be given,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Both more and less have given him the revolt,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: And none serve with him but constrained things

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Whose hearts are absent too.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Let our just censures

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Attend the true event, and put we on

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Industrious soldiership.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: The time approaches

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: That will with due decision make us know

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: What we shall say we have and what we owe.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Thoughts speculative their unsure hopes relate,

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: But certain issue strokes must arbitrate:

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Towards which advance the war.

stage_directions.text: Drum and colours. Enter Malcolm, old Siward and his Son, Macduff, Menteith, Caithness, Angus, Lennox, Ross, and Soldiers, marching.

glosses.definition: private rooms — where Scots will sleep safely once Macbeth is deposed

glosses.phrase: Let every soldier hew him down a bough
glosses.definition: Malcolm's masterstroke — each soldier cuts a branch to disguise the army's numbers; Birnam wood will literally come to Dunsinane
carry it in front of him

annotations.title: Birnam wood, literally
annotations.body:
Malcolm's order — 'Let every soldier hew him down a bough, / And bear't before him' — is the prophecy's literal fulfillment. The Sisters promised Macbeth could not be vanquished until Birnam wood came to Dunsinane; Malcolm's soldiers carry branches, and the wood moves. The equivocation the apparitions relied on — a literal impossibility becoming a literal truth — is here reversed by military strategy.

The tactic is real. Ancient and medieval armies used boughs to disguise troop numbers — there is a plausible historical precedent, though the particular incident is Shakespeare's invention (or Holinshed's, from whose *Chronicles* Shakespeare drew). The scene is ten lines long. It is the shortest scene in the play. Its function is to let the audience see the prophecy break, before Macbeth does.
sources: Geoffrey Bullough, *Narrative and Dramatic Sources of Shakespeare* vol. 7 (1973)

trials.prompt: Malcolm's 'hew him down a bough' tactic:
options: Is the literal fulfillment of the Birnam-wood prophecy — military strategy defeats equivocation
